metadata.split_role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
metadata.notes: Core/restored mushroom records retained at the original mushrooms.json path. Expanded mushroom records now load from smaller grouped files listed in split_manifest. v4.2.80 adds a core mushroom credibility pass: source links, use roles, foraging values, card notes, and cleaned placeholder use text for high-visibility mushrooms. v4.2.99-r2026-05-11-fold-mushroom-photo-patches1: folded mushroom photo patch records directly into canonical source records and retired runtime PHOTO_PATCH_PATHS. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved. v4.3.61-r2026-05-14-mushroom-completion-wave1-15species: completed selected mushroom species records in-place with rights metadata and stale review flags cleared.

split_manifest.role: Core/restored baseline mushroom records kept at the original path.
Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.
Expanded red/yellow, blue-staining, red-pored, and caution-lane bolete records.
Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.
Expanded long-stemmed, peppery, gilled-bolete, wood-inhabiting, and oddball bolete records.

review_reason_audit.rule: Normalize explicit review signals into review_reasons/review_note and prefix notes for Needs Review list visibility.

metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.
metadata.last_reaudit_note: Re-audited v4.3.61-v4.3.63 touched mushroom species against hard completion checklist; incomplete items explicitly declared instead of silently marked complete.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Good edible when properly identified and cooked; flavor is earthy/aromatic. Avoid old soggy specimens and confirm spore print/ID against purple Cortinarius look-alikes.
records.general_notes: A practical gilled entry for the guide because the color, smell, and late-season habit are all useful identification cues.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly fall after cool weather begins.

records.field_identification: Purple-lilac young mushroom fading brownish, with pinkish spore print, no veil, and growth in leaf litter or organic debris.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good cooked edible, but confirm it is a blewit and not a purple Cortinarius.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Clitocybe nuda
use_links.notes: Blewit color, spore print, odor, habitat, and Cortinarius comparison.

records.look_alike_notes: Purple Cortinarius species can look similar but have rusty-brown spore prints and often cobwebby cortina remnants; blewits have a pale pinkish-buff print and no cortina.
records.rights_correction_note: Photo-rights placeholders removed; image set limited to records with exact author/license metadata available.
records.edibility_detail: Edible only after confident identification and cooking. Confirm the pinkish-buff spore print and absence of a Cortinarius-style rusty brown spore print/cortina; avoid raw use, old soggy specimens, and any purple-brown mushroom with rusty spores or unpleasant earthy/chemical odor.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Not recommended for the table in this guide. Treat red pores plus blue staining as a stop sign unless confirmed by an expert.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food. Several red-pored or strongly blue-staining boletes can cause significant gastrointestinal illness, and beginner separation is unreliable.
records.general_notes: This is a safety grouping, not a single species. It catches the red-pored/blue-staining bolete lane so users do not accidentally treat all boletes as edible.

stem_feature: Often reticulate or dotted; variable by species
smell: Variable; avoid if unpleasant or uncertain
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Olive-brown to brown in many boletes
mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly summer into fall; treat as caution whenever found.

edible_use.notes: Avoid for food. Several red-pored or strongly blue-staining boletes can cause significant gastrointestinal illness, and beginner separation is unreliable.

records.photo_patch_basis: exact slug match in uploaded mushrooms.json
records.scientific_name: mixed red-pored / blue-staining bolete caution group
records.field_identification: Umbrella caution group for boletes with red to orange pore surfaces and/or rapid blue staining. Check pore color, bruising speed, reticulation on the stem, cap color, smell, and tree association. In this guide, red-pored or strongly blue-staining boletes are not food entries.
records.mushroom_card_note: Red pores or strong blue staining = caution lane. Do not eat from this group as a beginner.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus sensibilis
use_links.notes: Representative red/yellow pore, blue-staining caution bolete.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Frost's bolete
use_links.notes: Representative red-pored bolete used for caution-group comparison.

completion_audit.auditor_note: Species touched in v4.3.61-v4.3.63 was rechecked against the hard completion checklist. A record is only marked complete when all checklist items pass; otherwise the missing requirement is explicitly declared.

records.notes: Large ruffled mass with noodle-like folds rather than gills, pores, or teeth.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Use young, clean specimens. Slice or tear into ribbons after careful cleaning; good sautéed, in soups, or dried if clean enough.
records.general_notes: Eastern North American cauliflower mushrooms are often treated around the Sparassis spathulata/americana complex. Use as a field-group entry rather than overpromising one species name.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible when young, clean, and confidently identified. Dirt, bugs, and sand collect deep in the folds, so discard old, waterlogged, or hard-to-clean specimens.

underside: Wrinkled folds; no gills or pores
substrate: Near conifers or hardwoods depending species

records.photo_patch_group: Batch D — coral, jelly, and odd-form mushrooms
records.field_identification: Large cream to pale yellow mass of flattened, ruffled, noodle-like folds at the base of trees or from buried roots. It looks like a loose cauliflower or egg noodles, not an upright coral with many tiny branch tips.
records.mushroom_card_note: Ruffled noodle-like mass at tree bases; good when young and clean, but it can be a dirt-and-bug hotel.

edible_use.notes: Good edible when young, clean, and confidently identified. Dirt, bugs, and sand collect deep in the folds, so discard old, waterlogged, or hard-to-clean specimens.

completion_audit.auditor_note: Species touched in v4.3.61-v4.3.63 was rechecked against the hard completion checklist. A record is only marked complete when all checklist items pass; otherwise the missing requirement is explicitly declared.

records.notes: Irregular black charcoal-like mass on living birch with warm brown interior.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal and modern research interest, mostly as extracts/tea; evidence and safety vary, and it should not be treated as a cure.
records.culinary_uses: Used as a tea/decoction rather than a table mushroom. Harvest sparingly from living birch and avoid taking all material from one tree.
records.edibility_detail: Used for tea/extract traditions rather than as food.
records.general_notes: Not a culinary mushroom; better filed under medicinal/other uses despite being a fungus.
medicinalTerms: Kidney injury risk with heavy use

growth_form: Sterile conk / black mass on birch
mushroom_profile.season_note: Visible year-round on living birch trunks.

records.other_uses: Excellent practical tinder / ember carrier. The dry rusty-brown inner material catches a spark readily, smolders like a coal, and can help start or carry fire; scrape, crumble, or powder the inner material. If damp or freshly harvested, expose or dry the inner material first for best spark-catching.

medical_terms: Kidney injury risk with heavy use
medicinal.preparation_notes: Usually simmered or steeped as tea; stronger extracts/supplements are a different risk category than an occasional beverage.
medicinal.warnings: Use moderation. Chaga can be high in oxalates and case reports link heavy or long-term powder use with kidney injury. It may also increase bleeding risk with anticoagulant/antiplatelet drugs and may affect blood sugar, so avoid casual supplement-style use with kidney disease, bleeding disorders, diabetes drugs, blood thinners, pregnancy, or before surgery without medical guidance.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Inonotus obliquus
use_links.notes: Black sterile conk on birch and sterile/fertile body distinction.

use_links.label: Review article: Inonotus obliquus research background
use_links.notes: Research/background link only; not a treatment recommendation.

records.field_identification: Black, cracked, charcoal-like sterile conk protruding from living birch; interior is orange-brown, not wood.
records.look_alike_notes: Birch polypore and hoof fungus form recognizable brackets; chaga is a black, cracked sterile mass with orange-brown interior, usually on living birch.
records.edibility_notes: Tea / infusion. Woody conk is not eaten like a normal mushroom, but it is commonly simmered or steeped as a bitter tea / coffee-like beverage. Used for tea/extract traditions rather than as food.
curation_notes: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.

edible_use.notes: Woody conk is not eaten like a normal mushroom, but it is commonly simmered or steeped as a bitter tea / coffee-like beverage. Used for tea/extract traditions rather than as food.

records.mushroom_card_note: Birch conk used for tea/decoction; harvest conservatively and avoid cure-all thinking.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Detailed culinary and caution notes are still being copied back.
records.general_notes: Useful as a group entry because amateurs often meet the field concept before they sort out the exact Russula name. Flexible gills are a meaningful criterion here. Representative Commons images use Russula cyanoxantha to illustrate the mild flexible-gill charcoal-burner group.

records.notes: Bright orange and yellow shelving bracket with pores below, not true gills.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Young, tender shelves are a good edible mushroom after thorough cooking. Trim away tough bases and avoid old shelves; some people get stomach upset even from correctly identified chicken of the woods.
records.general_notes: Some people react to it, especially older specimens or those from some conifers.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible when young and tender, but not universally tolerated; avoid old, tough, bug-ridden, or conifer-grown material unless you know the local Laetiporus species well.

mushroom_profile.season_note: May through November in eastern hardwood records, but most typical in summer and fall; early spring finds are less common.

records.field_identification: Bright orange to yellow overlapping shelves growing from wood, with a pore surface underneath rather than gills. The texture is soft and fleshy when young, becoming tough with age; host tree and pore color help separate Laetiporus species.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good edible when young and cooked, but tolerance varies; confirm pores, wood growth, and host tree.

records.notes: White coral-like tooth fungus with many branches carrying hanging spines.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Hericium species have medicinal/supplement interest, but this entry is treated primarily as food.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible when young and white. Cook gently; older yellowing specimens lose quality.
records.general_notes: Another Hericium ally that benefits from side-by-side comparison with lion's mane and bear's head tooth.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible Hericium when young, white, and tender after confident identification. Confirm short teeth arranged along delicate branching structures on dead hardwood; avoid yellowing, brittle, dirty, or decaying specimens and separate it from bear’s head tooth and lion’s mane by branching and tooth placement.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer through fall on hardwood logs.

records.field_identification: Branched white Hericium with short teeth hanging in rows along the branches, usually on dead hardwood.
records.mushroom_card_note: Choice toothed edible; short teeth hang along delicate branches on hardwood.

records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Included only as a deadly Amanita caution record.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food in this guide; keep as an identification/caution record.
records.general_notes: One of the highest-priority caution mushrooms in the guide. The combination of white gills, ring, and volva matters more than almost anything else in the gilled lane.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in wet periods.

records.medicinal_uses: No medicinal use in this guide.
records.field_identification: White gilled Amanita with free white gills, white spore print, ring on the stalk, and a sack-like cup/volva around the base. The base may be underground, so the entire mushroom must be carefully dug up for ID.
records.mushroom_card_note: Deadly white Amanita: free white gills, ring, white spore print, and sack-like volva/cup at the base.

records.photo_patch_group: Batch E — poisonous and caution look-alike support
records.poisoning_effects: Deadly white Amanita group. Treat as life-threatening; amatoxin-type poisoning can cause delayed severe illness and organ injury.
records.overview: Deadly white Amanita caution stub for mushroom records that mention destroying angels as look-alikes.
records.look_alike_notes: Never identify white edible mushrooms without checking the entire base. A hidden cup/volva plus free white gills and a ring is an emergency-level warning pattern.

records.notes: Large tan-brown bracket with dark scales above and broad pores underneath.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when very young and tender; older caps become leathery. Good sliced thin, sautéed, or dried for stock when still fragrant.
records.edibility_detail: Edible young only; quality drops quickly with age.
records.general_notes: Mostly worth collecting young; older specimens get leathery fast.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Most useful as a young spring mushroom before it toughens.

records.field_identification: Large scaly tan-to-brown shelf with big angular pores and a watermelon-rind/cucumber-like odor.
records.mushroom_card_note: Fair edible only when young and tender; older pheasant backs are stock material at best.
